title: Requirement Gap Analysis
description: Final scene. The student is given the product spec before any testing starts and must identify where it is incomplete, contradictory, or missing acceptance criteria. No bugs to find in a running app — this is about reading a spec critically and thinking like a QA lead.
context: A second checkout flow feature is being planned. Before the team starts building, the QA lead has asked the SQA engineer to review the spec and flag any gaps, ambiguities, or contradictions that would make it impossible to write complete test cases. The spec is displayed in the SpecsTab.

spec_document.title: Checkout & Payment Registration — Product Spec v1.3

1_1.heading: User Registration
1_1.content: All users must register with a valid email address and password before completing a purchase. Characters must be inputted for the password field.

gaps.description: Password section says 'characters must be inputted' but never defines minimum length or complexity requirements. No character count, no special character rule, no uppercase requirement — nothing the team can write a test case against.

1_2.heading: Payment Processing
1_2.content: The checkout form collects card number, expiry date, and CVV. Payment is processed via Stripe. On successful payment the user is redirected to an order confirmation page.

gaps.description: Spec defines the happy path (successful payment → confirmation page) but says nothing about what happens when a payment is cancelled mid-flow, or when a Stripe token expires during checkout. No timeout handling or cancelled payment state is defined anywhere in the spec.

2_1.heading: Checkout Flow
2_1.content: The checkout flow must guide the user from cart to payment confirmation in as few steps as possible. The flow should minimise drop-off.

2_4.heading: Guest Checkout
2_4.content: Guest checkout must remain available to reduce checkout attrition for first-time buyers.

gaps.description: Section 1.1 states all users must register before purchasing. Section 2.4 states guest checkout must remain available. These directly contradict each other. The spec provides no resolution — no definition of how guest orders are stored, how guest users differ from registered users in the database, or whether guest users should be prompted to create an account post-purchase.
gaps.example_resolution: Allow guest checkout but prompt for account creation post-payment. Store guest orders under a temporary guest_user record that can be converted to a full account. Update Section 1.1 to read: 'Users must register or check out as a guest. Guest users are prompted to register after order confirmation.'

3_1.heading: Error Handling
3_1.content: The form must display appropriate error messages for invalid inputs.

medium.title: No cancelled payment or timeout state defined
medium.description: Spec defines the happy path (successful payment → confirmation page) but says nothing about what happens when a payment is cancelled mid-flow or when a Stripe token expires during checkout.
medium.business_impact: Without a defined cancelled or expired state, the team will make inconsistent implementation decisions. QA cannot write test cases for cancellation flows because there is no expected behaviour to test against.
medium.proposed_resolution: Add a section defining: (1) what the user sees if payment is cancelled, (2) whether a partial order record is created, (3) what happens if the Stripe session token expires mid-checkout (timeout duration + redirect behaviour).

medium.title: Password complexity not defined
medium.description: Section 1.1 says 'characters must be inputted' for the password field but provides no minimum length, no complexity rule, and no special character requirement.
medium.business_impact: Cannot write a test case with a pass/fail criterion. A single-character password technically satisfies 'characters must be inputted'. The spec cannot be tested as written.
medium.proposed_resolution: Define minimum password requirements explicitly — e.g. minimum 8 characters, at least 1 uppercase, 1 number. Add these as acceptance criteria in Section 1.1.

hard.title: No cancelled payment or timeout state defined
hard.description: Spec defines the happy path (successful payment → confirmation page) but says nothing about what happens when a payment is cancelled mid-flow or when a Stripe token expires during checkout.
hard.business_impact: Without a defined cancelled or expired state, the team will make inconsistent implementation decisions. QA cannot write test cases for cancellation flows because there is no expected behaviour to test against.
hard.proposed_resolution: Add a section defining: (1) what the user sees if payment is cancelled, (2) whether a partial order record is created, (3) what happens if the Stripe session token expires mid-checkout (timeout duration + redirect behaviour).

hard.title: Password complexity not defined
hard.description: Section 1.1 says 'characters must be inputted' for the password field but provides no minimum length, no complexity rule, and no special character requirement.
hard.business_impact: Cannot write a test case with a pass/fail criterion.
hard.proposed_resolution: Define minimum password requirements explicitly in Section 1.1.

hard.title: Spec contradiction: Section 1.1 (mandatory registration) vs Section 2.4 (guest checkout)
hard.description: Section 1.1 states all users must register before purchasing. Section 2.4 states guest checkout must remain available. These directly contradict each other. No resolution is provided.
hard.business_impact: If both sections are implemented literally, the system will have undefined behaviour — is a guest checkout order valid or not? Does it create a user record? This contradiction creates a security gap (unregistered user records) and a database consistency gap (guest orders with no associated user).
hard.proposed_resolution: Allow guest checkout but prompt for account creation post-payment. Store guest orders under a temporary guest_user record that can be converted to a full account. Update Section 1.1 to state: 'Users must register or complete checkout as a guest. Guest users are prompted to create an account after order confirmation.'

gap_analysis_report_template.note: Template is provided on easy mode only. Medium and hard modes have a blank input field.

bp_sqa_6.description: Student submits their requirement gap analysis report

analytical_reasoning.high: Identifies at least 2 of the seeded gaps (per difficulty level), explains the business impact of each gap on testing and on the product, and proposes a specific actionable resolution for each
analytical_reasoning.medium: Identifies at least 1 gap correctly with business impact, but proposed resolutions are vague or missing for one gap
analytical_reasoning.low: Only identifies the most obvious gap (the Section 1.1 vs 2.4 contradiction) or lists gaps without explaining why they matter

attention_to_detail.high: Correctly references the spec section number for each gap (e.g. 'Section 1.2 does not define...'). On hard mode: finds all 3 gaps including the implicit ones (cancelled payment, password complexity) not just the explicit contradiction.
attention_to_detail.medium: Finds the gaps but doesn't reference spec sections, or finds obvious gaps but misses the more implicit ones
attention_to_detail.low: Misses one or more of the seeded gaps entirely — particularly the cancelled payment state and the password complexity gap which require active inference rather than just reading

communication_clarity.high: Report is structured with each gap clearly separated, each with a title, location, description, impact, and resolution. Another person could act on this report without asking follow-up questions.
communication_clarity.medium: Report identifies the gaps but is written as flowing text rather than structured — harder to act on
communication_clarity.low: Report is a list of complaints with no structure, no proposed resolutions

decisiveness.high: On hard mode: proposes a concrete resolution for the Section 1.1 vs 2.4 contradiction (e.g. guest checkout allowed but with post-purchase account creation prompt) rather than just flagging it
decisiveness.medium: Flags the contradiction and says 'PM should resolve this' without proposing a specific resolution
decisiveness.low: Does not flag the contradiction, or flags it with no comment on what the resolution should look like

good_signals: identifies GAP-001 (missing cancelled payment / timeout state)
on hard: identifies GAP-003 (Section 1.1 vs 2.4 contradiction)
references the specific spec section for each gap
explains business impact — not just 'this is a gap' but 'this means we cannot write test cases for X'
proposes a specific actionable resolution for each gap
on hard: proposes guest checkout + post-purchase account creation prompt as the resolution for GAP-003
bad_signals: only finds GAP-003 (the obvious contradiction) and misses GAP-001 and GAP-002
lists gaps without explaining business impact
proposes no resolutions — just flags the problems
treats all gaps as equally critical with no prioritisation

easy.hint_text: Tip: Look for sections where there is no clear acceptance criterion — something you could not write a pass/fail test case for. Also look for sections that might contradict each other.
easy.note: Easy mode presents a clean spec — student is introduced to the format but no gaps to find. Scene ends after student reviews the spec and confirms no gaps found.

hard.dan_asks_for_resolution: I've read your gap report. On the Section 1.1 vs 2.4 conflict — I need an actual proposed resolution from you, not just a flag. What do you recommend we do?

strong_performance.condition: Student identifies 2+ gaps with business impact and proposed resolutions
strong_performance.outcome_label: Gap analysis approved — spec sent back to PM for revision before build starts

medium_performance.outcome_label: Partial gap analysis — PM asks for a follow-up pass on the spec

weak_performance.condition: Student finds no gaps or flags obvious issues with no proposed resolution
weak_performance.outcome_label: Gap analysis returned — QA lead asks student to re-read the spec and resubmit
